reviews.review: good

Too many enemy units, and the unit requisition system is too obtuse and unclear - and stingy - to allow you to even deal with the 2nd mission successfully. The morale system SUCKS and makes your outnumbered units even more useless.

I recommend this game but only if dramatically discounted in a sale or some such. It's fun. A steady storyline appropriate to the setting. Check out the gameplay in the vid to see the steady tile based style - combatwise it's similar to CIV games for tiles, range, damage etc.  But that's it. This is W40k. So there is only war....
If that ain't your cup of tea.... skip it

Excellent Game no problems on windows 11

only problem I have is loading maps from the map editor anybody know how?
Good turn based game, feels like your playing the tabletop.
This game is War! The scenarios and missions take some thought and skill. Challenging and fun.

I'd rate this neutral if that was possible.

It's a fun game if you don't mind the outdated aesthetic and having to look at a guide to work out what the difference is between all the Leman Russ tank variants ( https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=1089864462 ).

But because of that... it's not for everyone.

Most people would probably prefer Dawn of War (RTS, with many more factions and campaigns), Warhammer Total War (4x), or Battlesector (tactical)
old school  strategy at its finest . very good.

Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon is a fantastic game... The art, sounds, graphics, all are really fantastic. It reminds Me of a game from long ago called Warhammer 40,000: Final Liberation... It's like Panzer General 2 / Panzer Corps. had a baby with the Warhammer 40,000 Universe... The interactive story line, where You can pick what your Commander says to other characters is a great little touch as well. This game is a keeper, from a developer that has been impressing Me lately...
PanzerCorps+Warhammer40K = a very good match

It's pretty neat overall - fun combat and a generous selection of troops and vehicles.

The campaigns are fairly adequate so far, and there are many scenarios that act as one-off battles.

BUT - no skirmish mode to casually try out new armies!

This is actually a dealbreaker for some, god bless em, but personally I still recommend the game as it's quite fun if you are the sort of person who really enjoyed playing wh40k: Final Liberation.
Great for a rainy day or those moments when Warrggh! is within you LOL!!!!!
pretty fun, nice lil hex strat game

I would really like this game, but terrible balance, dead mod scene, and difficult modding (all just text editing, making edits very tedious) make this a no for me. Really, there are both bugs and crazy balance decisions that will drive you nuts when you notice.
Great turn-based strategy game

This is a bizarrely overpriced game for its production value. The mechanics are very solid, if sometimes a little over-complicated for little reward. there's not much going on visually, and the timer is a little too tight to allow you to do much other than aggressive manoeuvres.

Over 500 hours of playing time, at least. It's as addictive as Panzer General, but in the Warhammer universe. I only wish they had made more DLCs for this system, with other races and campaigns. Yeah, there's Battle Sector, and Dawn of War, but this system is tried and true and never disappoints. Gotta say, the aircraft are pretty much all glass cannons. Useful for scouting at most, definitely don't want to put them up against any full strength units. Whelp, gotta get back to playing. Still haven't beaten "Golgotha" yet...
Still buggy AF nearly 10 years after release, unbalanced, no unit info except stats and a story that seems to be missing pieces. I tried to like it but there are better ones out there
God this game is so good.
very nice tactical game, for Warhammer fans must have :)

disclaimer: i'm playing this after XCOM2 WotC which i consider one of the best games around so i might have somewhat high expectations at the moment.

Unlike my friend who for illustration would throw 7 sometimes with 6d dice :D So I have to drop this game as the only thing I was doing all the time was reload. Sorry but this is not working Flashback games and I wanted it to work so bad. How can I be ok when once I do with same attack 8 dmg and after reloading I do 0 dmg. I mean how can you plan tactically when sometimes you destroy that enemy and sometimes not. Don't tell me it is simulating life battle because that would be moronic statement. This would work as table top, not as computer game. I am so angry. This is simple a RELOADING GAME. I have better things to do with my time :P
And I was so looking forward to it. I still have such a good memories with old game Final Liberation. 
Must stop or I will puke.

I really really love this game! You can customize your army in the campaign to have a combination of units that really fits your play style. I like the storyline of the game and as someone who has at least some knowledge of 40K lore, it was interesting to see those lore elements at play in the game. The game runs smoothly and as far as I've seen there aren't any bugs. The game can be addicting, especially with the turn-based part that really makes planning very important, and I love that about the game. Highly recommend it!

But, it does feel like it's missing something.
They definitely could do more with it.
Could be cgear, Space Marine camigm  toom be fifficult. OK

Great game. I'm old enough to remember Avalon Hill's Panzer Blitz/Panzer Leader/ Arab-Israeli Wars. This is strongly reminiscent of those. I play this when I feel nostalgic
Very good strategy wargame for WH40k fans, despite some minor flaws.
Wanted to like it since the lore it covers, but i've seen better animations on NES. Request refunded
old school fun

Honestly not impressed. It's a standard WWII turned based hex battle engine, reskinned for 40k.
They've completely lost the Grimdark feel. Nothing feels special about any of the units, and it really doesn't feel like I'm fighting for the future of ... anyone. I found it so boring I barely made it though half a dozen scenarios before uninstalling.

If you enjoy the WW style strategy turn based games, then this might appeal as a 40k themed one.
But I can't recommend to anyone who likes other 40k style games.

One of the best games to come out of the 40k scene since THQ went under. This game puts you in the shoes of an Imperial Guard commander during the second war for Armageddon, which you fight from the first days of the war to the end. You gain access to a massive tech tree of Guardsmen, Space Marines, and even Titans by the game's end, all of which come with a veterancy system to discourage throwing away your troops needlessly. 

Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon delivers a very faithful look into the grim darkness of the far future, and I would recommend it to anyone who is a fan of 40k's setting.
Great game, similar to the the tabletop experience. Wide selection of units for every faction and engaging story.
Closest you can get to table topping and a lot cheaper.

This is a very good game indeed but as Slitherine are like paradox in milking their customers to death with DLC i waited until it was dirt cheap before purchasing this game for next to nothing like i always do with Paradox games you see devs there is a price to exploiting your customers instead if throwing big bucks at you they get wise to your antics and then exploit you back! So by pissing off your customers you make less money NOT MORE.
Still holds up in 2022. Played Battlesector recently and was looking for something in the same vein. Still in the tutorial but having a lot of fun. Pick it up during the Skull Sale.

Great atmosphere, gameplay and level design. I really enjoyed my playthrough of the campaign. It was challenging enough without being too brutal and there were some events that I did not see coming, which is rare in games these days.
Great
Addictive

Turn based hex system. If you don't like hex grid or tactics style game  then I would think twice about its purchase.

I like this game its enjoyable for me since i like the format and the warhammer theme with mission driven missions. 

Is it worth A$57?   No.  I feels dated in gameplay and style (well it was made in 2014) I personally think you should on;y purchase this game at a steep discount I would think A$15 - A$20 is a fair price. 

If you looking for RTS the best warhammer game for me was the Dawn of war series.

Edit: I want to be clear that I haven't actually played for 157 hours. When you quit the game, the launcher stays open and that contributes toward your total time played.

I've been playing Armageddon on the Very Hard difficulty for some time now and I can sum up all of gameplay very simply: Get a unit with a lot of vision to reveal hexes, blast it at range with artillery, then close in with your melee guys to finish them off.  That's the whole game. Sometimes you might spice things up with a defense mission where you throw in "support units" that shoot when adjacent units are hit, but for the most part that's it. 

Moreover, the AI is bad. As a defender, if you get Orks onto a bridge, you can permanently lock them in place by getting them down to 1 HP and then just running out the clock. Your units act as a road block, preventing forward movement and the AI won't shuffle crippled unis to the back of the line, nor will they attack if it means certain death. HOWEVER, the AI is ALSO too passive when you're attacking. If the AI doesn't have vision on you, it won't do anything (as in it skips the AIs whole turn), even as you hammer at it with artillery turn after turn. 

It makes for a boring, drawn out experience. Armageddon doesn't feel like a test of your military tactics, it feels like a game of rock paper scissor. There's an absolutely colossal number of units, but most of them are useless or not as good as their alternative. If you bring the right units, you win every time. If you bring the wrong units, you lose. 

The only saving grace is the story moments in between, but even those get tiresome after a while because it boils down to "Commander, great job doing that thing, but the Orks are relentless, so we need you to go do a thing." It's that for 30 missions, just slightly rephrased each time.
Simple but effective turn based strategy

I'm late to the party, having finally reviewed the game after six years on-and-off release. Firstly, when the game was first introduced, Matrix/Slitherine *didn't ever* have any sales. Times have changed, and you can likely get this gem for 75% during the coming year.

Also, check out the scenario design/modding thread here on Steam. There is an awesome 2.2 gig mod updated on 2/22 that works smoothly even on my seven-year-old lump of a computer. The game even looks better!

Designed, I believe, by the folks who did Panzer Corps 2; it's funny, but this is the better game overall. And I am not a WH enthusiast. With the mod, there are hundreds of specialized units available for further editing, etc. This is the best of the Matrix TBS WH franchise. Best wishes to all.

This is a great warhammer title.
Good gameplay, strategy, unit variety.
it's oldschool but boy is it worth it in my eyes.

This game is a hidden gem! I was not expecting much from this game and bought it cause I´m a fan of 40k and strategy games. When I first started the campaign I was taken aback for a moment, because the game has full voice acted lines for the briefing/story in the campaign, which I was not expecting especially from a slitherine published game (*cough panzer corps cough*). The game was really immersive because you can name the commander after you, and you are a new commander which has to prove himself on armaggedon hive prime. You have during the story some choices which alter the story (mostly battles). You can feel the desperate atmosphere of the last stand of the steel legion. You meet through the story Comissar Yarrick and other legendary 40k figures (I won´t say who cause I don´t want to spoil the game, but if you are familiar with the lore of the Wars for Armageddon you may already know who I am referencing). You will fight the Ork WAAAGH! and the Warlord Ghazghkull Thraka till your last men. You will have to sacrifice many of your soldiers. But in the end the EMPEROR PROTECTS!
I really wish they would make a second installment to this game with a fully voice acted campaign and choices you can make, be it trivial i don´t care.
BTW: there are 2 endings in the game, so be careful through your playthrough and watch out for heresy....

Possibly my favorite WH40k title.  In regards to the couple of reviews discussing the game being simple, I feel its as simple as you decide to make it.  

The AI is definitely not the best, its rather passive and prioritizes killing weak units it seems.  Its easy to just find the best units and exploit the AI with artillery and tanks.  But the game has given you hundreds of different units to play with.  

Experiment.  

Use the units that appear worse off.  

I wish there was a skirmish mode against the AI so you can test army compositions to your hearts content.  But, the campaign's various paths make it re-playable enough (for me at least).  

I really hope they make a sequel

This game has what one other reviewer called an "old-fashioned aesthetic." By 2022 standards, I suppose that this is true. The maps can be a little bit drab and the appearance of the units in this game won't have you writing home in excitement to your mother. What they are is perfectly serviceable and decent - if you like this kind of game. This game is very much a spiritual successor of the original Panzer General/Pacific General/Allied General and, perhaps most of all, Warhammer 40,000: Rites of War. If you loved Rites of War back in the day and still enjoy play turn-based war games of the beer-and-pretzel variety, you'll have a lot to love here. There are a lot of similarities between that game and this. Based on what I remember from that game, however, combat resolution is somewhat more complicated in this game and is different, in fact, from contemporary superficially similar games like Slitherine's Order of Battle or Panzer Corps. It bears spending a few minutes reading some of the helpful guides here on Steam or - even more so - going over to the Slitherine folders and reading some of the posts there regarding combat mechanics, army management, and so on.

This isn't the kind of game that will necessarily offer you infinite replay value in single-player mode - the AI is fairly programmatic and you won't find the AI engaged in truly novel behavior if you replay any of the missions. On the basic, normal setting, the AI is not terribly difficult once you learn how to manage this games particular version of combined arms, but remains, in my estimation, entertainingly capable of punishing the careless player. Nevertheless, as I wrap up the first campaign, I find that this game hits the marks of what I was really hoping for:  a successor game to Rites of War, which I really enjoyed.

One caveat: if you are a true tabletop Warhammer 40k lover, you might find yourself puzzled by how certain units function in relationship to each other (which is to say, they don't work at all as they do on the table). This doesn't bother me, but I also knew what I was getting into.
Good stuff, 7.5 out of 10.

It's a hard nope, quick refund on this one.
From the janky voice overs to the game-boy level graphics, this was a poorly executed butchery of what could have been a great game. Terribad UI, unchallenging combat, weak story.
Perhaps it was made in a retro style as some sort of homage/work of love, but after playing better strategy 40k games like gladius or adeptus mechanicus, you can't un-ring a bell and go back to this style very easily.

tl;dr: Would only recommend bought at reduced price and/or if you're nostalgic about Warhamemr Epic 40k or similar. However, I'm only in the first third of the game for now.

Edit: Change to reccomend, it gets better, story picks up a bit, feels like the battle goals varies more now.

I bought it on the cheap (I think 10€...?), even so I think it's decent at best. A lot of info seems to not be available. Having played the turtorial a lot is not explained, and I even find myself reading through the pdf manual to figure some of the stats out, but the manual is certainly not up-to-date:
 - Under "Examples of weapon statistics" a table is shown with some weapons having a range of 0, saying this means it can only attack units next to it. This is how range 1 functions. 
 - "Attacks per turn" is a stat, but it doesn't make a lot of sense. In-game a unit can only attack once, if it's for retaliating/firing back when attacked, it is my experience a unit can do so an infinite amount of times. So how does "ammo" and "attacks per turn" actually functino in the game? It seems to in-game be that "ammo" = amounts of shot per attack, it is as if these two stats in the manual have been merged in-game. It seems weird to call it "ammo" but it is nto somethign that can seemingly run out.
 - There are no aerial units in the manual, all other unit types are explained, but this, more unique unit type, is not.

For some games, learning by doing works great. In this sort of game it seems odd, more of a mistaken omision. 
What exactly does gaining XP do? It says it increases attacking and a smaller bonus to defence, however it does not explain in-game what it does. Seems weird it is not info that jsut pops up by hovering over the XP, plus the manual is a bit untrustworthy, obviously, so is that even what it does?

For the gameplay itself I think it gets a bit repetitive, but it's alright. It seems to be a bit of a shame you can do nothing to regain requisition points mid-fight, such as holding points, or defeating important targets. It is fun to experience some of the 1997 Final Liberation: Warhammer Epic 40,000, it's nostalgic in that way. However I'm missing the world campaign map from that game in this game. I miss the yanky and campy live action videos.
Great Warhammer 40k Epic game.

Why in the name of all that is holy would you include an archaic mechanic like turn limits in a game released in 2014!?  Nobody likes turn limits.  Nobody.

It is a very good game.

TL;DR: Despite its simple presentation this is an addictive turn based tactics game with surprising depth.  Between all the different unit types to the game's mechanics to the different scenarios I was drawn into this game and couldn't let go.  Despite all of this, I think the base game is worth $20 at most.  If hex-based turn based tactics and WH40K are your thing then you should check this game out.

Gameplay: On the surface it appears simple, with each unit getting one move and attack action.  Deeper then that are all the different stats, weapons systems and tile types units have to deal with.  This means there is a good variety of units, with some being more versatile and others being more specialized.  Each unit can have up to three weapons, which can be useful in different situations or enhance a specific role.  You also have to consider "strength" (how many units is in a stack, usually ork units have higher strength).  Units can be carried over from one scenario to the next (if they survive) and increase in experience.  This makes them perform better.  You can also "upgrade" a unit, which allows them to be swapped with another valid unit.  It has to be the same type of the same faction though.  There are hundreds of units across five factions, but most of these are upgrade or sidegrade options to a standard unit.  Scenarios can have one objective or changing objectives, usually its to capture or defend hexes, move here or kill this.  The game is difficult, even on normal difficulty.  Expect to get spanked until you understand how the game works.

Content:  The base game has a 5 scenario tutorial, 30 scenario main campaign and a handful of one-off scenarios.  Each scenario takes 15-60 minutes to complete, depending on the size and the objective.  Main campaign took me 25 hours to complete, but that includes the tutorial plus time spend reading the manual and experimenting with army builds.  The main campaign is fully voice acted and has dialogue during missions too.  There are a good amount of twists and turns to keep you interested.  One issue is that by the 3rd act the game looses its steam.  Most of the scenarios are "capture this" with no twists and the orks become passive, save for a couple scenarios.  Also, by the 3rd act, all of the units are unlocked so there are no more surprises in that regard.  Otherwise the campaign is great with good sized fights and will keep you engaged.

Presentation:  Graphics look like an HD remake of a 90's game.  Some people hate it but I love it, they did a good job.  There are no unit animations but there are good and distinct attack effects based on the type of attack.  This makes it easy to discern what attack is doing what.  It seems goofy at first to see models slide around the map but it did not impede my enjoyment since it helps to save time.  Music is good, more like background ambient and orchestral pieces that set a meditative mood, which fits with the visual presentation.  Environments look detailed but my only complaint is that there isn't much variety in the maps in the base game.  There are a handful of scenarios in the jungle and one or two inside a hive city but otherwise its mainly out in the wastelands.

Performance:  On my budget desktop this game ran smooth without issue.  I didn't experience any glitches save for one weird visual glitch in between missions.  I run on windows 10 and haven't had any issues launching the game.

Lets be honest, you don`t buy a Slitherine game to play a Slitherine game..you want access to the game engine so you can go get the mods that make a well thought out and enjoyable game.
I can only assume Slitherine have at this point given up and make the game engine and resources for the modders and throw out a half baked beta safe in the knowledge the modders will pick up the pieces and sort it out...its the only explanation that makes sense to me when you see some of the EPIC and basic errors in the game.
Don`t bother looking at this base game..go look at the mods available and make your decision on buying it based on if there are any mods you like as the base game is an abysmal joke.
Fucking old as shit but I love it

A nice attempt to capture the success of the of the old SSI "Five Star General" series, but it just doesn't feel right.  It lacks the historical depth of "Panzer General", the fantastical attitude of "Fantasy General", the elan of "People's General", and the immensity of "Star General".

An unfinished game that I would only recommend to die-hard 40k fans when on sale. Before buying, skim through this great guide by Cuddles to get an idea of how half-baked this game's systems are, and then decide if you still want it: 

https://steamcommunity.com/sharedfiles/filedetails/?id=1089864462

Sure it could be enjoyable if you're looking for just any sort of strategy experience with a 40k skin... But if you're really bothered by [u]nonsensical design choices[/u] (there is no mechanic for "blasts", basic infantry can shred aircraft) [u]incomplete systems[/u] (none of the aircraft in the game are actual "flyers" even though the movement type exists in the game files. They "hover" and they can't hover over cliffs or mountains. The manual if full of references to other cut mechanics) and [u]not being able to see basic information[/u] in game (how much does this terrain impair my movement? why does my weapon do this much damage? what is my morale doing to my unit?) then i would give this game a pass. It has potential, and maybe a big fan patch could make it a much better game, but as far as I know there's no such magic bullet.

10/10 would accidently spend 21 hours on the main menu
(good game though)

Well I've played 4 hours to date and it's a fun game that sinks you in. 4 hours just passed and it only felt like 30 minutes because I had so much fun playing. Tactically it's a challenge and I like that. Not too hard, not too easy. Why more people haven't picked up this game title is beyond me. For being Warhammer, and with Warhammer having a not so great reputation on Steam, this is a really good 40K tactical game. So from my point of view, you should pick up this game and have fun.

Panzer Generals/Corps retarded cousin.

This is not necessarily a bad game, but if you're looking for strategic depth you won't find it here. There's also a story in there somewhere, and some dialogue options, which apart from the case when you're choosing a mission, they don't seem to do anything.

The UI is terrible when it comes to managing your units, I found it really hard to figure out which units are the best because there's no way to sort them and you have to hover over attributes to find out what they are which is really annoying. The icon chosen for the upgrade button doesn't make any sense. Also I'm not exactly sure what unit experience does or how it's supposed to make the unit better. You can't overpower a unit like in the panzer games.

There's also a resource that doesn't do anything. Just accumulates and that's it. No idea what it's for.

On the battle map you can't tell if a unit is in cover or if it has any buff or debuffs. Damage estimates are confusing. I don't get the point of some unit types. Air units are not really air units, they're more like hover; they can't stack over another unit, they can't go over hills/mountains, they have the same range as a ground unit. Infantry, tanks and artillery is all I'm using.

If you're a W40k fan sure scratches an itch, but I'd only get it heavily discounted like I did. I'ts just not worth the full price.
Needs more races. Definitely fun, get it on sales
Great game ,fun to play can be very challenging
This game is a ton of fun! If you've played Panzer General or something similar, you know how the combat works. I'm having a blast!

I'm only 4 hours in at the time of this review but this game surprised me. There isn't much in the way of animations and I originally thought this would cause me to refund the game before I even played it. I was thankfully and unexpectedly surprised by how little the animations affect my immense enjoyment of this title. I am a WH40K fan as well as somebody who likes turn based and RTS gaming. If you're a fan of either of those pick it up. Lately I've felt that companies have been dishing out eye candy with a side of gameplay. This game is the exact opposite, which in all honesty, is a breath of fresh air.
fun little game poof all done in 10-15 hr's very short for the cost
A pale imitation of the original.  If you came here, like me, hoping for the persistent campaign mechanics of the old days and a dive into massive battles, you'll be disappointed.

What is;

Pros:
-A nice storyline, true to the spirit of WH40k.
-Large variety of units.
- Very good dlcs.

Cons:
-Graphics taken from the early '90s.
-Multiplayer system based on the PBEM ...

Conclusion:
-If you like tbs games and WH40k, buy it.
-If you like tbs games but not WH40k, there are better options. Buy it if you find it at a discount.

Score 6/10.
about the closest experience to playing the table  top
Yes!

If you've been thinking, "I'd like a game in the style of Panzer General, but set in the WH40K universe", look no further.  There's nothing especially brilliant about Armageddon, but it's cleanly executed and does satisfy the itch.  The campaign has a bit of variety and a surprise or two, but mostly it's a familiar dance of infantry and armor, taking and holding objectives.

(If you have no idea what Panzer General is, and you like turn-based strategy games, give this one a try.  If afterward you want something with a bit more depth, Panzer Corps is the spiritual successor to PG.)

If you're looking for something closer to the WH40K tabletop experience, check out Sanctus Reach.
Probably the closest you'll get to a Warhammer 40k tabletop game.
A very solid turn-based strategy game made for fans of both SSI's "General" series from way back, and for fans of Warhammer 40,000 universe. If you happen to be both, this is a jackpot.

[i]"He was an avalanche from an unexpected quarter. He was a thunderbolt from a clear sky."[/i]
— Commissar Yarrick on Ghazghkull Mag Uruk Thraka

[h1]INTRODUCTION[/h1] 
[b]"Warhammer 40,000: Armageddon"[/b] takes place during the [b]Second War for Armageddon[/b], and is the second of three massive conflicts between the [b]Imperium of Man[/b] and the Ork Warboss, [b]Ghazghkull Mag Uruk Thraka[/b] that took place between 941.M41 through 943.M41. The war was fought on the Hive World of Armageddon in the Segmentum Solar, where Ghazghkull Thraka launched an [b]Ork WAAAGH![/b] consisting of five separate Ork tribes in an attempt to seize the planet for themselves.

Why is Armageddon so important to the Orks? It was once the planet of Ullanor, an Ork stronghold world that was initially crushed by Primarch Horus Lupercal, where he received the title Warmaster from the Emperor - opening the path to Lorgar's treacherous corruption of Horus, the Emperor's favoured son. 
It once again became the 'homeworld' of sorts for five enormous Ork "Beasts" in M32 that launched an invasion of Terra, and saw the original Imperial Fists wiped out to a man (the chapter was rebuilt with volunteers from successor chapters). 
After the Ork's defeat, Ullanor was said to be destroyed, but was instead ransacked by the Adeptus Mechanicus, transported to the Segmentum Solar using Ork technology and renamed Armageddon. 

But shhh! The Imperium doesn't know this.

Three Space Marine Chapters were involved in the second war, including: the Blood Angels (under Lord Commander Dante), the Salamanders and the Ultramarines, along with dozens of regiments of the Astra Militarum, as well as a large contingent of Inquisition forces from the Ordo Xenos composed of Squats. A notable effect of this invasion was that after the war, Sebastion Yarrick, the "Hero of Hades Hive" (a Commissar of the Officio Prefectus) attempted to follow through on his oath, that he would not rest until the day Ghazghkull Mag Uruk Thraka was hunted down and killed. He set off in pursuit of his nemesis in a game of cat-and-mouse that lasted for nearly ten standard years. Ghazghkull fled before Yarrick and a company of Mordian Iron Guard. The Ork Warboss protected what remained of his forces, only committing to rearguard actions when necessary. Yarrick attempted to break the deadlock by forcing his opponent down on the once-Imperial planet of Golgotha. What happened next....*spoilers*.

This is all covered in the final DLC [b]"Golgotha"[/b] (a very challenging set of scenarios where you the player fight at company level with core units you must look after well), but is essential to complete the tale.

The Second War for Armageddon saw also the transformation of the Librarian Brother Calistarius, presumed dead after falling to the Black Rage (or Red Thirst) and "dying" under the rubble of a cathedral. Rising anew after battling the Black Rage for seven days and seeing an image of Sanguinius raising him high, Calistarius was no more. In his stead stood Mephiston, the Lord of Death, second now only to Lord Commander Dante and seen as Dante's eventual successor.

But, that is a tale for another time.

[b]NOTE:[/b] Due to the fact that the history of the First War for Armageddon against Chaos has been entirely suppressed by the actions of the Inquisition, most of the Imperium, and the citizens of Armageddon itself, considered this Second War to be the First War for Armageddon, rather than the second.  


[h1]REVIEW[/h1] 
Based upon the now ageing [b]"Panzer Corps"[/b] engine, the game is still enthralling as you get to choose, from Act 2 on whether you keep your initial core of Astra Militarum Steel Legion under your command, via Commissar Sebastion Yarrick of the Officio Prefectus (aka. "The Old Man"), or if you wish to include Blood Angels and Ultramarines (Salamanders come in the Third Acts). The vast armouries under the overall command of Lord Commander Dante of the Blood Angels are open to the player, including all the Adeptus Astartes have to offer, and the Steel Legion ranks including Titans later in the game. 

It is a Turn-Based Strategy game, where the goal on each map is to seize objectives and wipe out your opponent, or hold your objectives against waves of Ork tribal warriors and their ricketty though deadly machines. 
You the player, are "The Commander" tasked with increasingly important missions, your exploits growing in story panels between missions, from critiques by the traitor Governor von Straub to exhortations to hold vital sites by Lord Commander Dante, to aggressively snatching Ork holdings by Yarrick. Whew!

It could be thought that the maps in thee game are quite ugly and make the game look bland of the ugliness of the maps, but this is Armageddon, a burnt and blistered hive world covered in irradiated deserts and equatorial jungles. It is ugly yes, but also fitting. The equatorial jungle maps do break it up somewhat, though.

Some of the DLC's add such units as the Space Marines separately with their own mini campaigns, while others such as [b]"Ork Hunters"[/b] introduce missions set in the jungles following on from the main campaign, that is broken into Acts. All of the DLC are expansions to the main game, adding new missions and units as mentioned and the game really is incomplete without them all. For the full experience, the Second Battle for Armageddon where Yarrick made his name and the legend was born you need the whole package. While you might think, especially early on where you only have Steel Legion forces (the Astra Militarum based on Armageddon) that the battles are the same, and 'grindy', once the Space Marines land and you get to include Ultramarines and Blood Angels (the sons of Vulkan appear and have their own mini campaign) the once mighty and indestructible Ork force start to whither. 
Send a Blood Angel Death Company (those taken by the Black Rage) force at a Morkanaut and see what happens. The entire Armouries of the Adeptus Astartes are open to you the player.
Aside from the Adeptus Astartes and the Steel Legion troopers, you also have Titans, under the command of Princeps Prime Kurtiz Mannheim, the commander of the [b]Adeptus Mechanicus' Legio Metalica Titan Legion[/b] that was temporarily stationed on Armageddon. 
Basically you have it all. It is a very long, and often very challenging war especially on the higher difficulty settings.

[h1]CONCLUSION[/h1] 
It is expected that there will be a [b]"W40K: Armageddon 2"[/b] based on the [b]"Panzer Corps 2"[/b] engine, but if it will just be a rehash of this war or go onto the Third War for Armageddon remains to be seen. My vote is for the Third War as you have The Steel Legion, Salamanders, Space Wolves, Black Templars, Celestial Lions, Sisters of Our Martyred Lady and Titans, the war made famous in the Black Library novel and fan YouTube video series of the same name, [b]"Helsreach"[/b].

Despite it's ageing engine, drab maps reminiscent of [b]"THH: Battle of Tallarn[/b], and requirement for much time investment to get the best experience, [b]'W40K: Armageddon"[/b] is an essential purchase for all [b]Warhammer 40,000[/b] fans and those who wish to explore this incredible war that led to Yarrick ("The Old Man") becoming a legend and the only Commissar that all Space Marines truly respect.
cool turn based game. I like the story.
